environmental_storytelling.example: New York City, late 1920s Prohibition era; Al Capone reference grounds supernatural plot in recognizable historical context
Denim-clad bikers wielding scimitars in 1920s NYC creates deliberate incongruity signaling supernatural/cult influence corrupting normalcy
H.P. Lovecraft Cthulhu Mythos elements embedded in environment and enemy design; Hastur as ancient god; bloodline mechanics from literary canon
Anett Myer's Peruvian sorceress identity and magical heritage visually distinguish her from 1920s urban setting; signals outsider protagonist

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Distinct visual designs; require combination of boomerang and spell attacks; mark level completion and progression milestones
Increase in density and complexity across levels; visual manifestation of occult alignment
Armed combat; varied enemy types; period-appropriate weapons and aesthetics
Spell acquisition and casting; boomerang combat; narrative-driven progression against dual threats

notes: El Viento communicates world-state primarily through narrative framing and enemy type variety rather than explicit visual systems. The 1920s setting contrasts with supernatural/Lovecraftian elements, creating tension that reflects the plot's convergence of organized crime and occult forces. Faction identity relies on enemy visual design (period criminals vs. supernatural cultists) rather than explicit color-coding or sigils. The spell progression system signals player empowerment necessary to face escalating threats. Limited source material constrains detailed extraction of specific visual manifestation techniques; inferences drawn from gameplay mechanics and narrative context provided in Wikipedia entry.
